evals.prompt: I need to write a simple character device driver for a custom FPGA peripheral. The FPGA is memory-mapped at a fixed physical address 0xA0000000, 4KB size, and exposes a single 32-bit register at offset 0x00 that I can read and write from userspace. No interrupts. I want userspace to access it via /dev/fpga0. I'm targeting Linux 6.6 on an ARM64 SoC. Can you write me a complete driver for this?
evals.expected_output: Complete, compilable Linux kernel module implementing a misc character device with ioremap, file operations (open/release/read/write or ioctl), ioremap of fixed MMIO address, a Makefile, and correct module boilerplate. Should use devm_ where applicable, include proper error handling in probe, and produce a /dev/fpga0 node via misc_register().

assertions.name: compiles-correctly
assertions.description: The driver uses correct Linux kernel headers and APIs for a 6.6-era driver (no deprecated APIs like check_mem_region)

assertions.name: uses-misc-register
assertions.description: Driver registers with the misc subsystem via misc_register() to create /dev/fpga0

assertions.name: uses-ioremap-or-devm-ioremap
assertions.description: Driver maps the MMIO region using ioremap_resource or devm_ioremap and uses readl/writel (not direct pointer derefs)

assertions.name: private-state-struct
assertions.description: Driver defines a per-device private struct (not global variables) to hold MMIO base and device state

assertions.name: file-operations-implemented
assertions.description: Driver implements file_operations with at least open, release, and either read/write or unlocked_ioctl

assertions.name: makefile-present
assertions.description: Response includes a working Makefile for out-of-tree module build

assertions.name: module-boilerplate
assertions.description: Driver includes MODULE_LICENSE, MODULE_AUTHOR, MODULE_DESCRIPTION macros

assertions.name: error-handling-in-init
assertions.description: Initialization/probe path checks return values of ioremap and misc_register and handles errors

evals.prompt: I'm writing a platform driver for a temperature sensor peripheral on an embedded SoC. The sensor is I2C-connected (address 0x48), described in Device Tree, and reports temperature as a 16-bit signed value in units of 1/128 °C. I want to expose it via the Linux IIO subsystem so that standard tools like iio_readdev can read it. Kernel 6.1. Show me the full driver including DT binding example, probe/remove, IIO channel registration, and the read_raw callback.
evals.expected_output: Complete I2C + IIO driver with: i2c_add_driver registration, of_device_id match table with MODULE_DEVICE_TABLE, probe() using devm_ APIs including devm_iio_device_alloc and iio_device_register, an iio_chan_spec array defining a temperature channel, read_raw callback converting the 16-bit sensor value to IIO format (val + val2 for millidegrees), and a DTS snippet showing the device node. Correct error handling throughout.

assertions.name: uses-i2c-driver
assertions.description: Driver uses i2c_add_driver() or module_i2c_driver() and defines struct i2c_driver

assertions.name: of-device-id-table
assertions.description: Driver defines of_device_id match table with MODULE_DEVICE_TABLE(of, ...)

assertions.name: uses-iio-subsystem
assertions.description: Driver uses devm_iio_device_alloc() and iio_device_register() to register with IIO

assertions.name: iio-channel-defined
assertions.description: Driver defines at least one iio_chan_spec with type IIO_TEMP

assertions.name: read-raw-callback
assertions.description: Driver implements read_raw callback that returns IIO_VAL_INT_PLUS_MICRO or IIO_VAL_FRACTIONAL for temperature

assertions.name: dts-snippet-present
assertions.description: Response includes a Device Tree node snippet showing compatible string, reg address, and example usage

assertions.name: devm-used-in-probe
assertions.description: Probe function uses devm_ APIs (devm_kzalloc, devm_iio_device_alloc) rather than manual allocation

assertions.name: temperature-conversion-correct
assertions.description: read_raw correctly interprets the 16-bit signed value in 1/128°C units and converts to millidegrees or appropriate IIO format

evals.prompt: I'm adding runtime power management to an existing platform driver. Right now the driver has probe/remove and basic hardware init, but it has no PM at all. The hardware has two power states: fully on (normal operation) and clock-gated (idle, register state preserved). Show me how to add both system sleep (suspend/resume for hibernate/S3) and runtime PM so the hardware gates its clock when idle for more than 50ms. I want to understand what each piece does, not just get boilerplate.
evals.expected_output: Clear explanation of system sleep vs runtime PM distinction, then code showing: dev_pm_ops with SYSTEM_SLEEP_PM_OPS and RUNTIME_PM_OPS macros, suspend/resume callbacks saving/restoring state, runtime_suspend/runtime_resume callbacks for clock gating, pm_runtime_set_autosuspend_delay(dev, 50) + pm_runtime_use_autosuspend() in probe, pm_runtime_enable/disable in probe/remove, pm_runtime_get_sync/put usage pattern in driver ops, and pm_ptr() usage on the driver struct. Should explain WHY each piece exists, not just show the code.

assertions.name: explains-system-vs-runtime-pm
assertions.description: Response clearly explains the difference between system sleep (S3/hibernate) and runtime PM (idle clock gating)

assertions.name: dev-pm-ops-correct
assertions.description: Driver defines dev_pm_ops using SYSTEM_SLEEP_PM_OPS and RUNTIME_PM_OPS macros (or equivalent SET_SYSTEM_SLEEP_PM_OPS / SET_RUNTIME_PM_OPS)

assertions.name: autosuspend-delay-set
assertions.description: Probe sets autosuspend delay to 50ms using pm_runtime_set_autosuspend_delay() and pm_runtime_use_autosuspend()

assertions.name: pm-runtime-enable-in-probe
assertions.description: Probe calls pm_runtime_set_active() and pm_runtime_enable(); remove calls pm_runtime_disable()

assertions.name: get-sync-put-pattern
assertions.description: Response shows pm_runtime_get_sync() before hardware access and pm_runtime_put_autosuspend() or pm_runtime_put() after, with error handling

assertions.name: pm-ptr-used
assertions.description: Driver struct uses pm_ptr(&mydriver_pm_ops) rather than a bare pointer, for CONFIG_PM=n compatibility

assertions.name: explains-why
assertions.description: Response explains the rationale behind key design choices (e.g., why devm_ order matters, why pm_runtime_get_sync returns negative, what EPROBE_DEFER means for PM)
